retrieved_docs.title: Intracranial Hypertension -- Treatment / Management -- Surgical interventions
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical interventions are typically reserved for patients at risk of vision loss. A high-volume lumbar puncture can be performed to decrease ocular pressures and temporize until a more definitive surgical intervention can be achieved. Possible surgical interventions include ventriculoperitoneal shunt and venous sinus stenting. Lateral ventricle volumes in patients with IIH are often small, and intraoperative image guidance can assist in accurate placement.

retrieved_docs.title: Pneumocephalus -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Intraoperative pneumocephalus can occur during cranial intervention owing to various risk variables, including: Concurrent brain atrophy Loss of the CSF negative-pressure gradient due to CSF egress Semisitting surgical position [29] The anatomic location and portal of air entry divide the condition into the following categories:

retrieved_docs.title: Intracranial Pressure Monitoring -- Personnel
retrieved_docs.content: Necessary personnel includes a composite healthcare team comprised of the following: A neurosurgeon A qualified assistant An attending nurse An anesthetist

retrieved_docs.title: Increased Intracranial Pressure -- Treatment / Management -- Invasive interventions
retrieved_docs.content: Invasive interventions are often required when medical management fails.

retrieved_docs.title: Increased Intracranial Pressure -- Treatment / Management -- Optic nerve sheath fenestration and CSF diversion procedures
retrieved_docs.content: In carefully selected patients, surgeons may also perform optic nerve sheath fenestration to relieve pressure and preserve vision, particularly in those with chronic idiopathic intracranial hypertension. [23] When elevated ICP results from hydrocephalus, surgical interventions, eg, ventriculoperitoneal or lumboperitoneal shunting, provide long-term CSF diversion.

retrieved_docs.title: Increased Intracranial Pressure -- Treatment / Management -- Lumbar puncture
retrieved_docs.content: A lumbar puncture can provide a temporary reduction of ICP in cases without mass effect. Clinicians must avoid the procedure in the presence of midline shift or obstructive hydrocephalus, as the sudden alteration in pressure may trigger brain herniation.

retrieved_docs.title: Ventriculoperitoneal Shunt -- Personnel
retrieved_docs.content: Neurosurgeon Anesthesiologist Nurse (circulating and technical)

retrieved_docs.title: Increased Intracranial Pressure -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the etiologies of increased intracranial pressure. Evaluate patients with the clinical features of increased intracranial pressure. Implement the treatment of increased intracranial pressure. Apply interprofessional team strategies to improve care coordination and outcomes for patients affected by increased intracranial pressure. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Intracranial Pressure Monitoring -- Clinical Significance -- Noninvasive Methods of ICP Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: Biodegradable pressure sensors are also being explored. [12] [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Intracranial Pressure Monitoring -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Strain gauge- or fiber-optic-based systems inserted into the ventricles or brain parenchymas are more accurate than fluid-coupled or pneumatic devices. Parenchymal monitors are easier to insert in midline shift or malignant brain swelling cases, with no risks of blockage by hemorrhage or debris. However, they cannot be re-calibrated in vivo; they can only measure localized pressure and have drift issues in long-term usage. The current non-invasive techniques are not accurate enough to replace traditional invasive techniques.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 684
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures constitute a broad operative category in which direct visual or instrumental assessment of an anatomic region is performed, often together with therapeutic interventions, and are organized under the parent clinical‑action set “Clinical actions A‑G.” In the endocrine system, adrenalectomy—partial or complete, performed transabdominally, laparoscopically, dorsally, or via a lumbar approach, with or without biopsy—serves as an exploration procedure and may include excision of adjacent retroperitoneal tumors. Hepatobiliary exploration comprises cholecystectomy with common bile duct exploration and may be combined with cholangiography, choledochoenterostomy, transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty, followed by choledochotomy or choledochostomy for drainage, calculus removal, or cholecystotomy. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 437
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures constitute a broad surgical category within the “Clinical actions A‑G” hierarchy that encompasses operative techniques designed to investigate, decompress, or remove pathology across multiple anatomic regions. In endocrine surgery, partial or complete adrenalectomy—performed transabdominally, laparoscopically, or dorsally—and adrenal gland exploration (with or without biopsy) may be combined with excision of adjacent retroperitoneal tumors. Hepatobiliary exploration includes cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration, cholangiography, choledochoenterostomy, transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty, choledochotomy or choledochostomy with drainage, calculus removal, and optional cholecystotomy. Neurosurgical exploration comprises infratentorial (posterior‑fossa) and supratentorial craniectomy or craniotomy, suboccipital craniectomy for cranial‑nerve decompression, and transcranial orbital exploration with lesion excision. Spinal exploration is achieved

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 97
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures constitute a broad surgical category that encompasses organ‑specific and region‑specific operative techniques used to investigate, diagnose, and treat pathology across multiple anatomical sites. Within this category, adrenalectomy (partial or total) may be performed via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal approaches and can include biopsy or excision of an adjacent retroperitoneal tumor. Cholecystectomy combined with common‑bile‑duct exploration includes standard duct exploration, cholangiography‑guided exploration, choledochoenterostomy, and transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty, with or without cholangiography; similar principles apply to choledochotomy or choledochostomy procedures that incorporate exploration, drainage, or calculus removal. Cranial exploration is represented by exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy, subdivided into infratentorial (posterior‑fossa) and supratentorial approaches, as well as suboccipital craniectomy for cranial‑nerve

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1959
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures constitute a broad category of surgical interventions that are employed to examine, diagnose, or treat pathology in diverse anatomic regions, often incorporating both definitive therapeutic steps and optional adjunctive techniques. In the endocrine and retroperitoneal domain, partial or complete adrenalectomy—or adrenal gland exploration with or without biopsy—can be performed via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal approaches, and may include excision of adjacent retroperitoneal tumors as a possibly included method. Hepatobiliary exploration is represented by cholecystectomy combined with common duct exploration, which may be accomplished with intra‑operative cholangiography, choledochoenterostomy, or laparoscopic techniques, each listed as a primary method. Neurosurgical exploration includes infratentorial (posterior fossa) craniectomy or craniotomy performed for diagnostic or therapeutic purposes, as well as transcranial orbit exploration for lesion removal.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 97
retrieved_docs.content: is represented by exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy, subdivided into infratentorial (posterior‑fossa) and supratentorial approaches, as well as suboccipital craniectomy for cranial‑nerve decompression; orbital lesions may be removed through a transcranial approach. Spinal exploration includes cervical, thoracic, lumbar, and sacral laminectomy/laminotomy techniques that provide exploration and/or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy; single‑segment procedures involve one to two vertebral levels, whereas multi‑segment procedures involve more than two levels. Excision is the operative method underlying many of these exploration procedures and serves as the parent concept for “Clinical actions A‑G,” which in turn parents the broader Exploration procedure class. Excision is applied to partial or complete adrenalectomy; cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration (including all cholangiographic and sphincterotomy variants);

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 97
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedure is defined as a broad surgical category that encompasses operative techniques used to investigate, decompress, or remove pathology across multiple anatomic regions. Within this category are adrenalectomy (partial or complete) performed transabdominally, lumbarly, or dorsally, with or without adrenal biopsy and often combined with excision of an adjacent retroperitoneal tumor; cholecystectomy with common bile duct exploration (standard, cholangiography‑guided, choledochoenterostomy, or transduodenal sphincterotomy/sphincteroplasty) and choledochotomy/choledochostomy with exploration, drainage, or calculus removal (with or without transduodenal sphincterotomy); cranial exploration comprising exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy in infratentorial (posterior fossa) and supratentorial compartments, suboccipital craniectomy for cranial‑nerve decompression, and transcranial orbit exploration with lesion removal; spinal exploration represented by laminectomy procedures

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 2487
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is a broad surgical category that includes a variety of operative methods while explicitly excluding several spinal decompression techniques. Procedures classified as methods of Excision comprise Adrenalectomy, partial or complete, or exploration of adrenal gland with or without biopsy, transabdominal, lumbar or dorsal (separate procedure) and Adrenalectomy, partial or complete, or exploration of adrenal gland with or without biopsy, transabdominal, lumbar or dorsal (separate procedure); with excision of adjacent retroperitoneal tumor. Cholecystectomy with exploration of common duct (procedure) and Cholecystectomy with exploration of common duct with cholangiography (procedure) are also methods of Excision, as is the laparoscopic variant Laparoscopy, surgical; cholecystectomy with exploration of common duct. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 207
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures constitute a broad surgical category used to investigate, decompress, or treat pathology across multiple anatomic regions and are organized by specific operative methods and, when appropriate, by adjunct techniques; they are nested under the parent clinical grouping “Clinical actions A–G.” In the adrenal gland, partial or complete adrenalectomy performed via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal approaches—and sometimes accompanied by biopsy or removal of adjacent retroperitoneal tumors—is classified as an exploration procedure. Hepatobiliary exploration includes cholecystectomy with common‑duct examination (with or without cholangiography, choledochoenterostomy, or transduodenal sphincterotomy/sphincteroplasty) and choledochotomy/choledochostomy with exploration, drainage, or calculus removal, optionally combined with cholecystotomy and sphincter procedures. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1187
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures encompass a broad range of operative techniques used to investigate, decompress, or remove pathological tissue in various anatomical regions. In the endocrine and abdominal domains, adrenalectomy—whether partial, complete, or performed with adrenal gland exploration and optional biopsy via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal approaches—is classified as an exploration method, as are cholecystectomy procedures that incorporate common duct exploration with choledochoenterostomy, or with transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty, with or without cholangiography. Choledochotomy or choledochostomy with exploration, drainage, or calculus removal, optionally combined with transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty, is also listed as a possible component of exploration surgery. Neurosurgical exploration includes both craniectomy and craniotomy performed for investigative purposes, with infratentorial (posterior fossa) and supratentorial variants identified as

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 4 -- Community 2757
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures represent a broad category of surgical interventions in which direct visual or instrument‑guided examination of an anatomic region is combined with therapeutic action. In the biliary system, cholecystectomy with exploration of the common duct can be performed as a stand‑alone procedure, as a laparoscopic cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration, or as part of a more extensive operation that includes transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty, with or without cholangiography. A related biliary exploration, choledochotomy or choledochostomy with exploration, drainage, or calculus removal, may also incorporate transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty, indicating that these adjunctive techniques are possibly included within the primary exploration. Cranial exploration procedures include supratentorial craniectomy or craniotomy performed for diagnostic or therapeutic purposes, as well as suboccipital craniectomy aimed at exploring or decompressing

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 2 -- Community 1215
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures represent a broad category of surgical interventions that involve direct examination, dissection, or removal of pathology across many organ systems and anatomical regions. Within this category, adrenalectomy—whether partial, complete, or performed with adrenal gland exploration and optional biopsy via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal approaches—may also include excision of adjacent retroperitoneal tumors. Cholecystectomy procedures frequently incorporate exploration of the common bile duct, either with intra‑operative cholangiography, choledochoenterostomy, or laparoscopic techniques, highlighting the integration of biliary tract assessment into gallbladder surgery. Neurosurgical exploration includes craniectomy or craniotomy for supratentorial lesions and transcranial orbit exploration with lesion removal, underscoring the role of direct intracranial and orbital access in managing complex cranial pathology. Spinal exploration is performed through various

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 878
retrieved_docs.content: part of an exploration procedure. Laparoscopic surgery also provides a method for biliary exploration, exemplified by Laparoscopy, surgical; cholecystectomy with exploration of common duct. Cranial exploration is represented by procedures such as Craniectomy or craniotomy, exploratory; infratentorial (posterior fossa) and Craniectomy, suboccipital; for exploration or decompression of cranial nerves. An additional cranial technique is Exploration of orbit by transcranial approach with removal of lesion, which targets orbital pathology through a transcranial route. Spinal and cauda equina exploration is achieved through a series of laminectomy techniques that may be performed without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy. These include Laminectomy with exploration and/or decompression of spinal cord and/or cauda equina for 1–2 vertebral segments in the lumbar region (excluding spondylolisthesis), a comparable lumbar‑sacral variant for 1–2 segments, and extended versions for more than

These are listed as “has_possibly_included_method” for Exploration procedure and include “Laminectomy with exploration and/or decompression of spinal cord and/or cauda equina, without facetectomy, foraminotomy or discectomy (eg, spinal stenosis), 1 or 2 vertebral segments; sacral,” “Laminectomy … 1 or 2 vertebral segments; thoracic,” and “Laminectomy … more than 2 vertebral segments; thoracic.” Additional operative methods classified under Exploration procedure are “Laparoscopy,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 1087
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is presented as a broad surgical category that includes a wide variety of operative techniques. It is the method used for arthroscopic removal of loose bodies from a joint, and it also serves as the operative approach for several biliary procedures, such as cholecystectomy with exploration of the common duct combined with choledochoenterostomy, cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration plus transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty (with or without cholangiography), and laparoscopic cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration. Excision is likewise the designated method for multiple cranial surgeries, including exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of the infratentorial (posterior fossa) region, exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of the supratentorial region, and suboccipital craniectomy performed for exploration or decompression of cranial nerves. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 2 -- Community 1132
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedure is classified as a clinical action within the broader group “Clinical actions A – G.” It serves as an umbrella term for a wide range of operative methods that are performed to investigate, expose, or treat anatomical structures. Specific methods that are directly identified as “has_method” of Exploration procedure include several variants of cholecystectomy with common duct exploration—namely “Cholecystectomy with exploration of common duct with cholangiography (procedure),” “Cholecystectomy with exploration of common duct with choledochoenterostomy,” and “Cholecystectomy with exploration of common duct; with transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty, with or without cholangiography.” The category also encompasses cranial exploratory surgeries such as “Craniectomy or craniotomy, exploratory; infratentorial (posterior fossa),” “Craniectomy or craniotomy, exploratory; supratentorial,” and “Craniectomy, suboccipital; for exploration or decompression of cranial

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 125
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures constitute a broad surgical category that includes organ‑specific and region‑specific operative techniques performed to examine, diagnose, or treat pathology. Within the endocrine system, partial or total adrenalectomy and adrenal‑gland exploration (transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal approaches) with or without biopsy and possible excision of an adjacent retroperitoneal tumor are classified as exploration. Hepatobiliary exploration comprises cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration, cholangiography, choledochoenterostomy, and transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty, as well as choledochotomy or choledochostomy for drainage or calculus removal. Neurosurgical exploration includes infratentorial and supratentorial craniectomy or craniotomy, suboccipital craniectomy for cranial‑nerve decompression, and transcranial orbital exploration with lesion removal.
